day1.topic: Place Value: Reading & Writing Numbers up to 1 Billion
objectives: Read and write numbers up to 1 billion in words and figures
Identify the value of each digit in a large number
Compare two large numbers using >, < or =
Skip count forwards and backwards in 25s, 50s and 250s

concepts.heading: Place Value Columns
concepts.body:
Every digit in a number has a PLACE VALUE — its value depends on its position.
The columns from right to left are:
Units, Tens, Hundreds, Thousands, Ten Thousands, Hundred Thousands, Millions, Ten Millions, Hundred Millions, Billions.

Example: In 2,735,000,000 — the digit 7 is in the Hundred Millions column, so its value is 700,000,000.
concepts.note: A billion = 1,000,000,000. Count the zeros: there are 9 of them.

concepts.heading: Comparing Large Numbers
concepts.body:
To compare two numbers, start from the LEFTMOST digit and move right until you find a difference. The number with the bigger digit in that column is larger.

Example: Compare 5,223,487,637 and 5,113,487,637.
Both start with 5 (billions). Then 223 million vs 113 million — 223 > 113.
So: 5,223,487,637 > 5,113,487,637
concepts.note: Always use the correct symbol: > means "greater than", < means "less than", = means "equal to".

concepts.heading: Skip Counting
concepts.body:
Skip counting means adding (or subtracting) the same number each time.

Counting forward in 250s from 1,000:
1,000 — 1,250 — 1,500 — 1,750 — 2,000 — 2,250...

Counting backward in 25s from 10,000:
10,000 — 9,975 — 9,950 — 9,925 — 9,900...

worked.q: Write 1,295,800,000 in words.
worked.a: One billion, two hundred and ninety-five million, eight hundred thousand.

worked.q: What is the value of the digit 7 in the number 2,735,000,000?
worked.a: 7 is in the Hundred Millions column. Its value = 700,000,000 (seven hundred million).

worked.q: Which is greater: 8,301,456,221 or 8,310,456,221? Write using the correct symbol.
worked.a:
Compare from left: both have 8 billion. Then 301 million vs 310 million. 310 > 301.
8,301,456,221 < 8,310,456,221

day3.topic: Rounding: Whole Numbers, Decimals & Significant Figures
objectives: Round whole numbers to the nearest 10, 100, 1000, 10,000 and 100,000
Round decimal numbers to the nearest tenth, hundredth and thousandth
Distinguish between rounding up, rounding down and rounding off
Express numbers to given significant figures

concepts.heading: Rounding Off (the most common type)
concepts.body:
Look at the digit IMMEDIATELY to the RIGHT of the place you are rounding to.
• If that digit is 5 or more → round UP (increase the target digit by 1)
• If that digit is 4 or less → round DOWN (keep the target digit the same)
Then replace all digits to the right with zeros.

Example: Round 2,846,655 to the nearest thousand.
Look at the hundreds digit: 6 (which is ≥ 5) → round up.
Answer: 2,847,000
concepts.note: Round UP = always take the larger option. Round DOWN = always take the smaller option. These ignore the digit rules.

concepts.heading: Rounding Decimals
concepts.body:
The same rule applies to decimals. Look at the digit one place to the RIGHT.

Example: Round 486.3685 to:
• Nearest tenth (1 d.p.): look at hundredths digit = 6 (≥5) → 486.4
• Nearest hundredth (2 d.p.): look at thousandths digit = 8 (≥5) → 486.37
• Nearest thousandth (3 d.p.): look at ten-thousandths digit = 5 (≥5) → 486.369
concepts.note: d.p. means decimal places. 1 d.p. = tenths. 2 d.p. = hundredths. 3 d.p. = thousandths.

concepts.heading: Significant Figures (s.f.)
concepts.body:
Count from the FIRST NON-ZERO digit. That is your first significant figure.

Example: Express 857,386,321 to 3 significant figures.
First 3 digits: 8, 5, 7. The next digit is 3 (< 5) → round down.
Answer: 857,000,000

For decimals: 0.00234567 to 3 s.f.
First non-zero digit is 2. Count: 2, 3, 4. Next digit is 5 → round up.
Answer: 0.00235
concepts.note: Trailing zeros after a decimal point ARE significant. 0.360 has 3 significant figures.

worked.q: Round 2,846,655 to (a) the nearest thousand (b) the nearest hundred thousand
worked.a:
(a) Hundreds digit = 6 (≥5) → round up: 2,847,000
(b) Ten-thousands digit = 4 (<5) → round down: 2,800,000

worked.q: Round 0.08475 to (a) 1 decimal place (b) 2 decimal places (c) 3 decimal places
worked.a:
(a) Hundredths digit = 8 (≥5) → round up: 0.1
(b) Thousandths digit = 4 (<5) → round down: 0.08
(c) Ten-thousandths digit = 7 (≥5) → round up: 0.085

worked.q: Express 857,386,321 to 4 significant figures.
worked.a: First 4 digits: 8, 5, 7, 3. Next digit = 8 (≥5) → round up the 3 to 4. Answer: 857,400,000

day5.topic: Mental Maths: Multiply & Divide by Powers of 10, Word Problems
objectives: Multiply and divide numbers by 10, 100 and 1000 mentally
Apply doubling, halving and the distributive property to solve problems
Solve word problems using mental mathematics strategies
Identify key words in problems: sum, difference, product, quotient

concepts.heading: Multiplying and Dividing by Powers of 10
concepts.body:
× 10: shift the decimal point ONE place to the RIGHT
× 100: shift TWO places to the RIGHT
× 1,000: shift THREE places to the RIGHT
÷ 10: shift ONE place to the LEFT
÷ 100: shift TWO places to the LEFT
÷ 1,000: shift THREE places to the LEFT

Examples:
105.25 × 1,000 = 105,250
105.25 ÷ 100 = 1.0525
0.075 × 10,000 = 750
concepts.note: Think of the decimal point as fixed and the digits moving. Or think of the decimal point moving.

concepts.heading: Doubling, Halving & Distributive Property
concepts.body:
DOUBLING & HALVING: to make multiplication easier, halve one number and double the other.
Example: 28 × 5 → halve 28, double 5 → 14 × 10 = 140

DISTRIBUTIVE PROPERTY: split a number into parts that are easier to multiply.
Example: 18 × 6 → think (20 - 2) × 6 = (20 × 6) - (2 × 6) = 120 - 12 = 108
Example: 7 × 15 → think 7 × (10 + 5) = 70 + 35 = 105
concepts.note: These tricks save time in exams. Practise them until they feel automatic.

concepts.heading: Key Words in Word Problems
concepts.body:
Recognise the operation from the language used:

ADDITION: sum, total, altogether, increased by, more than
SUBTRACTION: difference, less than, reduced by, how much more, remaining
MULTIPLICATION: product, times, of (with fractions), multiplied by
DIVISION: quotient, share equally, how many times, divided by, per

worked.q: Use the distributive property: 9 × 98
Word problem: Mrs Adamu bought 13.6 kg of meat. Mrs Anderson bought 2.4 kg less. How many kg did they buy altogether?

day7.topic: Index Notation: Powers of Numbers, HCF & LCM
objectives: Understand and use index notation (powers of numbers)
Express numbers as products of prime factors
Apply the zero index rule: any number⁰ = 1
Find the Highest Common Factor (HCF) and Lowest Common Multiple (LCM)

concepts.heading: Index Notation — Powers of Numbers
concepts.body:
When we multiply the same number by itself repeatedly, we write it using a POWER (index).
2 × 2 × 2 × 2 × 2 = 2⁵ = 32

The BASE is 2. The EXPONENT (index) is 5.
2⁵ is read as "2 to the power of 5".

More examples:
3⁴ = 3 × 3 × 3 × 3 = 81
5³ = 5 × 5 × 5 = 125
10⁴ = 10,000
concepts.note: THE ZERO INDEX RULE: any number to the power of zero = 1. So 7⁰ = 1, 100⁰ = 1, 1,000,000⁰ = 1.

concepts.heading: Product of Prime Factors
concepts.body:
Every whole number can be written as a product of PRIME numbers. Use a factor tree.

Example: Express 72 as a product of prime factors.
72 = 8 × 9 = (2 × 2 × 2) × (3 × 3) = 2³ × 3²

Example: Express 48 as a product of prime factors.
48 = 2 × 24 = 2 × 2 × 12 = 2 × 2 × 2 × 2 × 3 = 2⁴ × 3

concepts.heading: HCF and LCM
concepts.body:
HIGHEST COMMON FACTOR (HCF): the largest number that divides exactly into two or more numbers.
Method: Express both as products of primes. Multiply the COMMON factors (using the lowest power).

Example: HCF of 24 and 30.
24 = 2³ × 3. 30 = 2 × 3 × 5.
Common: 2 and 3. HCF = 2 × 3 = 6.

LOWEST COMMON MULTIPLE (LCM): the smallest number that is a multiple of two or more numbers.
Method: Multiply ALL prime factors, using the HIGHEST power of each.

Example: LCM of 12 and 8.
12 = 2² × 3. 8 = 2³.
LCM = 2³ × 3 = 24.
concepts.note: HCF is used in sharing/cutting problems. LCM is used in "when will they meet again" problems.

worked.q: Express 32 as a power of 2. Then evaluate 5³ and 3⁴.
Find the HCF of 36 and 48 using prime factors.
Baba exercises every 12 days and Serwa every 8 days. They both exercise today. After how many days will they exercise together again?

objectives: Convert between fractions, decimals and percentages
Compare and order fractions with different denominators
Add and subtract fractions including mixed numbers
Solve word problems involving fractions

concepts.heading: Converting Between Fractions, Decimals and Percentages
concepts.body:
FRACTION → DECIMAL: divide the top by the bottom.
3/8 = 3 ÷ 8 = 0.375

DECIMAL → PERCENTAGE: multiply by 100.
0.375 × 100 = 37.5%

PERCENTAGE → FRACTION: write over 100 and simplify.
38% = 38/100 = 19/50

Benchmark fractions to memorise:
1/2 = 0.5 = 50%
1/4 = 0.25 = 25%
3/4 = 0.75 = 75%
1/3 = 0.333... = 33.3%
1/5 = 0.2 = 20%

concepts.heading: Comparing and Ordering Fractions
concepts.body:
Method 1: Convert to decimals and compare.
Method 2: Find the LCD (Lowest Common Denominator) and compare numerators.

Example: Which is greater, 7/12 or 8/10?
7/12 = 0.583... 8/10 = 0.8 → 8/10 is greater.

To order 5/6, 3/4, 7/8 in descending order:
5/6 ≈ 0.833, 3/4 = 0.75, 7/8 = 0.875
Order: 7/8 > 5/6 > 3/4

concepts.heading: Adding and Subtracting Fractions
concepts.body:
STEP 1: Find the LCD (Lowest Common Denominator)
STEP 2: Convert each fraction to an equivalent fraction with the LCD
STEP 3: Add or subtract the numerators. Keep the denominator.
STEP 4: Simplify if possible.

Example: 3/4 + 5/6
LCD of 4 and 6 = 12
3/4 = 9/12. 5/6 = 10/12.
9/12 + 10/12 = 19/12 = 1 7/12

For MIXED NUMBERS: add/subtract whole numbers first, then fractions.
concepts.note: Always simplify your final answer. If the answer is an improper fraction (top > bottom), convert to a mixed number.

worked.q: Order from least to greatest: 0.832, 3/8, 38%
Add: 2⅖ + 1⅔
A board is 12¼ feet long. A piece of 3⅓ feet is cut off. How long is the remainder?

objectives: Multiply a fraction by a whole number and by another fraction
Divide a fraction by a whole number and by another fraction using the reciprocal
Find a fraction of a quantity (money, length, weight)
Solve word problems involving multiplication and division of fractions

concepts.heading: Multiplying Fractions
concepts.body:
To multiply fraction × fraction: multiply the numerators together and the denominators together, then simplify.
Example: 2/3 × 3/5 = (2×3)/(3×5) = 6/15 = 2/5

To find a fraction OF a quantity, multiply:
Example: 2/3 of GH₵60 = 2/3 × 60 = GH₵40

To find a percentage of a quantity:
Example: 28% of 40 = 28/100 × 40 = 11.2
concepts.note: 'OF' means multiply. So '3/4 of 80' = 3/4 × 80 = 60.

concepts.heading: Dividing Fractions — The Reciprocal Method
concepts.body:
To divide by a fraction, multiply by its RECIPROCAL (flip the fraction upside down).
Example: 3 ÷ 1/4 = 3 × 4/1 = 12
Example: 5/8 ÷ 1/2 = 5/8 × 2/1 = 10/8 = 5/4 = 1¼

The reciprocal of 3/5 is 5/3. The reciprocal of 4 is 1/4.
concepts.note: Keep the first fraction. Change ÷ to ×. Flip the second fraction. Then multiply.

worked.q: There are 132 learners in a school. 2/3 are girls. How many boys?
A stack of plates weighs 10 kg. Each plate weighs 1/4 kg. How many plates?

day13.topic: Ratios: Language, Unit Rate & Equivalent Ratios
objectives: Write and simplify ratios using the correct language and notation
Calculate unit rates from given information
Find missing values in equivalent ratio tables
Solve sharing problems using ratio

concepts.heading: What is a Ratio?
concepts.body:
A ratio compares two or more quantities of the same kind. It can be written as: a:b or a/b or "a to b".

Example: In a class of 60 boys and 120 girls:
Ratio of boys to girls = 60:120 = 1:2 (simplify by dividing both by HCF = 60)

IMPORTANT: Order matters. Boys:Girls = 1:2 is NOT the same as Girls:Boys = 2:1.

To simplify a ratio: divide both parts by their HCF.
Example: 45:75. HCF = 15. Simplified: 3:5.
concepts.note: A ratio has NO units — convert both quantities to the same unit first before writing the ratio.

concepts.heading: Unit Rate
concepts.body:
A unit rate compares a quantity to ONE unit of another quantity.

Example: If 2 litres of cola cost GH₵18:
Unit rate = GH₵18 ÷ 2 = GH₵9 per litre

Use the unit rate to find other values:
• Cost of 1.5 litres = 1.5 × 9 = GH₵13.50
• Cost of 7 litres = 7 × 9 = GH₵63

concepts.heading: Sharing in a Ratio
concepts.body:
To share an amount in a given ratio:
Step 1: Add the ratio parts to find the total number of parts.
Step 2: Divide the amount by the total parts to find one part.
Step 3: Multiply each ratio number by one part.

Example: Kofi and Ama share GH₵360 in ratio 3:5.
Total parts = 3 + 5 = 8. One part = 360 ÷ 8 = GH₵45.
Kofi = 3 × 45 = GH₵135. Ama = 5 × 45 = GH₵225.
concepts.note: Check: 135 + 225 = 360 ✓. Always verify your answer adds back to the original amount.

worked.q: Write the ratio 1.5 m to 60 cm in its simplest form.
worked.a: Convert to same units: 1.5m = 150cm. Ratio = 150:60. HCF = 30. Simplified = 5:2.

worked.q: If 3 kg of meat costs GH₵60, find the cost of 7 kg.
worked.a: Unit rate = 60 ÷ 3 = GH₵20/kg. Cost of 7 kg = 7 × 20 = GH₵140.

worked.q: Kafui (36), Adoley (48) and Jantuah (24) share money in ratio of their ages. Jantuah gets GH₵24,000. How much altogether?
worked.a: Ratio = 36:48:24 = 3:4:2. Total parts = 9. Jantuah = 2 parts = GH₵24,000. 1 part = GH₵12,000. Total = 9 × 12,000 = GH₵108,000.

objectives: Use proportional reasoning to solve missing value problems
Express one quantity as a percentage of another
Find percentage increase and decrease
Calculate simple interest using the formula

concepts.heading: Proportional Reasoning
concepts.body:
Two quantities are in proportion if their ratio stays the same.
Direct proportion: as one increases, the other increases by the same factor.

Example: 5 kg costs GH₵40. Find cost of 8 kg.
Unit rate: 40 ÷ 5 = GH₵8/kg. Cost of 8 kg = 8 × 8 = GH₵64.

Cross-multiplication method:
5/40 = 8/x → 5x = 40 × 8 = 320 → x = 64

concepts.heading: Percentage: Express, Increase & Decrease
concepts.body:
Express A as a % of B: (A ÷ B) × 100
Example: Express 45 as a % of 180: (45 ÷ 180) × 100 = 25%

PERCENTAGE INCREASE:
% increase = (increase ÷ original) × 100
Example: Price rises from GH₵80 to GH₵100. Increase = 20. % increase = (20 ÷ 80) × 100 = 25%

PERCENTAGE DECREASE:
% decrease = (decrease ÷ original) × 100
Example: Salary drops from GH₵500 to GH₵400. Decrease = 100. % decrease = (100 ÷ 500) × 100 = 20%

concepts.heading: Simple Interest
concepts.body:
Simple Interest (SI) = Principal × Rate × Time ÷ 100
Or: SI = P × R × T / 100

Example: Invest GH₵1,500 at 8% per year for 2 years.
SI = 1500 × 8 × 2 ÷ 100 = GH₵240
Total amount = 1500 + 240 = GH₵1,740

To find the Principal if given SI, Rate and Time:
P = SI × 100 ÷ (R × T)
concepts.note: Simple interest is calculated on the ORIGINAL amount (principal) only. It does not compound.

worked.q: A woman saves GH₵520 at 6% interest for 1 year. How much interest does she earn?
A shirt was GH₵120. It is now GH₵150. What is the percentage increase?
Express 75 as a percentage of 300.

objectives: Identify and extend number and shape patterns
Find the rule for a sequence and express it algebraically
Use input-output tables to find and apply rules
Plot points on a number plane from a table of values

concepts.heading: Number Patterns & Rules
concepts.body:
A pattern is a sequence where each term follows a RULE.

ARITHMETIC SEQUENCE: add or subtract the same number each time.
Example: 3, 7, 11, 15... → add 4 each time. Rule: 4n - 1
Term 1 = 4(1)-1 = 3. Term 10 = 4(10)-1 = 39.

GEOMETRIC SEQUENCE: multiply or divide by the same number each time.
Example: 2, 6, 18, 54... → multiply by 3 each time.
concepts.note: To find the nth term of an arithmetic sequence: rule is dn + (first term - d), where d = common difference.

concepts.heading: Input-Output Tables & the Number Plane
concepts.body:
A mapping rule takes an input (x) and produces an output (y).
Example: Rule x → 2x + 1
x: 0, 1, 2, 3, 4
y: 1, 3, 5, 7, 9

The number plane has an x-axis (horizontal) and y-axis (vertical).
Each point is written as (x, y).
• (3, 5): 3 right, 5 up
• (-2, 4): 2 left, 4 up
• (0, -3): on y-axis, 3 down
concepts.note: To find a missing INPUT from a given output: work BACKWARDS. If rule is x→3x+1 and output is 22: 3x+1=22, 3x=21, x=7.

worked.q: Continue the pattern and find the 10th term: 3, 7, 11, 15...
worked.a: Common difference = 4. Rule: 4n - 1. Terms continue: 19, 23, 27, 31, 35, 39. 10th term = 4(10)-1 = 39.

worked.q: Find the rule for this table and complete it: Input: 1,2,3,4,5 | Output: 4,7,10,13,?
worked.a: Output increases by 3. Rule: 3x + 1. Check: 3(1)+1=4 ✓. Output for x=5: 3(5)+1 = 16.

worked.q: Triangles made of sticks: Pattern 1=3, Pattern 2=5, Pattern 3=7. How many sticks in Pattern 20?

objectives: Translate word statements into algebraic expressions
Simplify algebraic expressions by collecting like terms
Evaluate expressions by substituting given values
Write expressions for perimeter and area problems

concepts.heading: Writing Algebraic Expressions
concepts.body:
An algebraic expression uses letters to represent unknown numbers.

Translating words into algebra:
• "5 more than x" → x + 5
• "3 less than x" → x - 3
• "4 times x" → 4x
• "half of x" → x/2
• "2 more than 5 times x" → 5x + 2
• "x squared minus 3" → x² - 3

Real-life example: Afrako is 3 years older than Maako. Maako is x years old. Afrako's age = x + 3.
concepts.note: Always define what your variable represents. "Let x = the unknown number" or "Let x = Maako's age".

concepts.heading: Simplifying: Collecting Like Terms
concepts.body:
LIKE TERMS have the same variable and power — they can be added or subtracted.
UNLIKE TERMS have different variables — they CANNOT be combined.

Examples:
• 4x + 3x = 7x ✓ (like terms)
• 4x + 3y = 4x + 3y ✗ (unlike — cannot simplify)
• 5x + 4 - 2x - 4 = 3x
• 7xy + 5x - 4x + 2xy - 3 = 9xy + x - 3

Process: Group like terms first, then combine.
concepts.note: xy and yx are the same term. 2xy + 3yx = 5xy.

concepts.heading: Evaluating Expressions
concepts.body:
To EVALUATE: substitute the given values in place of the variables and calculate.

Example: Evaluate 3xy + 2x - y when x = 2, y = 3.
= 3(2)(3) + 2(2) - 3
= 18 + 4 - 3
= 19

Always follow BODMAS: Brackets, Orders/powers, Division, Multiplication, Addition, Subtraction.
concepts.note: When substituting negative numbers, use brackets to avoid sign errors: if x = -2, then x² = (-2)² = 4 (NOT -4).

worked.q: Write as an expression: "When 8 times x is subtracted from 5, the result is multiplied by 2."
Simplify: 5x + 4 - 9y + 3x + 2y - 7
worked.a: x terms: 5x+3x=8x. y terms: -9y+2y=-7y. Constants: 4-7=-3. Answer: 8x - 7y - 3

worked.q: Evaluate 3xy × 5y when x = 2, y = 4.

day1.topic: Numbers to 1,000,000: Place Value, Reading & Ordering
objectives: Read and write numbers up to 1,000,000 in figures and words
Identify the value of each digit using a place value chart
Compare and order large numbers using >, < and =
Round numbers to the nearest 10, 100, 1,000 and 10,000

concepts.heading: Place Value up to 1,000,000
concepts.body:
Every digit in a number has a value based on its POSITION.
Columns from right to left:
Units | Tens | Hundreds | Thousands | Ten Thousands | Hundred Thousands | Millions

Example: 725,463
7 → Hundred Thousands (700,000)
2 → Ten Thousands (20,000)
5 → Thousands (5,000)
4 → Hundreds (400)
6 → Tens (60)
3 → Units (3)

In words: Seven hundred and twenty-five thousand, four hundred and sixty-three.
concepts.note: A million = 1,000,000. It has SIX zeros.

concepts.heading: Rounding Large Numbers
concepts.body:
Look at the digit immediately to the RIGHT of the place you are rounding to.
• If it is 5 or more → round UP
• If it is 4 or less → round DOWN (keep the digit the same)

Example: Round 348,726 to the nearest thousand.
Look at hundreds digit: 7 (≥5) → round up.
Answer: 349,000

Example: Round 348,726 to the nearest ten thousand.
Look at thousands digit: 8 (≥5) → round up.
Answer: 350,000

worked.q: Write 483,057 in words.
worked.a: Four hundred and eighty-three thousand and fifty-seven.

worked.q: What is the value of the digit 6 in 364,819?
worked.a: 6 is in the ten-thousands place. Value = 60,000 (sixty thousand).

worked.q: Round 527,364 to (a) the nearest thousand (b) the nearest hundred thousand.
worked.a: (a) Hundreds digit = 3 (<5) → round down: 527,000. (b) Ten-thousands digit = 2 (<5) → round down: 500,000.

day3.topic: Multiply & Divide Large Numbers: Mental & Written Methods
objectives: Multiply and divide whole numbers by 10, 100 and 1,000 mentally
Multiply a 3-digit number by a 2-digit number using long multiplication
Divide a 3- or 4-digit number by a 1- or 2-digit number
Solve word problems involving multiplication and division

concepts.heading: Multiplying & Dividing by Powers of 10
concepts.body:
× 10: add one zero (or shift decimal one place right)
× 100: add two zeros
× 1,000: add three zeros
÷ 10: remove one zero (or shift decimal one place left)
÷ 100: remove two zeros
÷ 1,000: remove three zeros

Examples:
347 × 100 = 34,700
82,000 ÷ 1,000 = 82
56 × 1,000 = 56,000

concepts.heading: Long Multiplication (3-digit × 2-digit)
concepts.body:
Example: 347 × 26

Step 1: Multiply 347 × 6 (units):
6×7=42 (write 2, carry 4)
6×4=24+4=28 (write 8, carry 2)
6×3=18+2=20
Result: 2,082

Step 2: Multiply 347 × 20 (tens — put a zero first):
347 × 2 = 694, so 347 × 20 = 6,940

Step 3: Add: 2,082 + 6,940 = 9,022
concepts.note: Always write a zero placeholder before multiplying by the tens digit.

worked.q: A school orders 24 boxes of exercise books. Each box contains 144 books. How many books in total?

objectives: Find equivalent fractions by multiplying or dividing numerator and denominator
Simplify fractions to their lowest terms using the HCF
Compare and order fractions with different denominators using the LCD
Convert between improper fractions and mixed numbers

concepts.heading: Equivalent Fractions & Simplifying
concepts.body:
EQUIVALENT FRACTIONS represent the same amount.
To find equivalent fractions: multiply OR divide both numerator and denominator by the SAME number.

Example: 2/3 = 4/6 = 8/12 = 10/15

To SIMPLIFY: divide both numerator and denominator by their HCF.
Example: Simplify 24/36.
HCF of 24 and 36 = 12.
24 ÷ 12 = 2. 36 ÷ 12 = 3.
Answer: 2/3
concepts.note: A fraction is in its SIMPLEST FORM (lowest terms) when the HCF of numerator and denominator is 1.

concepts.heading: Comparing, Ordering & Mixed Numbers
concepts.body:
To COMPARE fractions: find the LCD and compare numerators.
Example: Which is greater, 3/4 or 5/7?
LCD = 28. 3/4 = 21/28. 5/7 = 20/28.
21 > 20, so 3/4 > 5/7.

IMPROPER FRACTION: numerator > denominator (e.g. 11/4)
MIXED NUMBER: whole number + fraction (e.g. 2¾)

Convert improper to mixed: 11/4 → 11 ÷ 4 = 2 remainder 3 → 2¾
Convert mixed to improper: 2¾ → (2×4) + 3 = 11 → 11/4

worked.q: Find three fractions equivalent to 2/5.
Simplify 36/48 to its lowest terms.
Convert 19/5 to a mixed number and 3⅔ to an improper fraction.

objectives: Add and subtract fractions with different denominators including mixed numbers
Multiply fractions and find fractions of quantities
Divide fractions using the reciprocal method
Solve word problems involving all four operations with fractions

concepts.heading: Adding & Subtracting Fractions
concepts.body:
Step 1: Find the LCD.
Step 2: Convert to equivalent fractions.
Step 3: Add or subtract numerators.
Step 4: Simplify.

Example: 3/4 + 5/6
LCD = 12. 3/4 = 9/12. 5/6 = 10/12.
9/12 + 10/12 = 19/12 = 1 7/12

For mixed numbers: add/subtract whole parts, then fractions.
Example: 4⅔ - 2¾
Convert: 14/3 - 11/4. LCD=12.
56/12 - 33/12 = 23/12 = 1 11/12

concepts.heading: Multiplying & Dividing Fractions
concepts.body:
MULTIPLYING: multiply numerators together and denominators together.
Example: 2/3 × 4/5 = 8/15
Simplify BEFORE multiplying where possible (cross-cancelling).

DIVIDING: Keep the first fraction. Change ÷ to ×. Flip the second fraction.
Example: 3/4 ÷ 2/5 = 3/4 × 5/2 = 15/8 = 1⅞

Finding a fraction OF a quantity:
2/5 of 80 = 2/5 × 80 = 32
concepts.note: 'OF' means MULTIPLY. Always simplify your final answer.

worked.q: Add: 2⅖ + 1⅔
Multiply: 3/8 × 4/9
A farmer has 6¾ acres of land. He uses ⅔ of it for maize. How many acres is that?

objectives: Convert between fractions, decimals and percentages
Add, subtract, multiply and divide decimal numbers
Calculate percentage of a quantity, percentage increase and decrease
Solve word problems involving decimals and percentages

concepts.heading: Converting Between Fractions, Decimals & Percentages
concepts.body:
FRACTION → DECIMAL: divide top by bottom
3/8 = 3 ÷ 8 = 0.375

DECIMAL → PERCENTAGE: multiply by 100
0.375 × 100 = 37.5%

PERCENTAGE → FRACTION: write over 100 and simplify
45% = 45/100 = 9/20

Key benchmarks:
½ = 0.5 = 50%
¼ = 0.25 = 25%
¾ = 0.75 = 75%
⅕ = 0.2 = 20%
⅓ ≈ 0.333 ≈ 33.3%
⅛ = 0.125 = 12.5%

concepts.heading: Percentage Increase, Decrease & Finding Amounts
concepts.body:
% of a quantity: (% ÷ 100) × quantity
Example: 35% of GH₵480 = 0.35 × 480 = GH₵168

% INCREASE: (increase ÷ original) × 100
Example: Price rises from GH₵120 to GH₵150.
Increase=30. %=(30÷120)×100=25%

% DECREASE: (decrease ÷ original) × 100
Example: Salary drops from GH₵800 to GH₵640.
Decrease=160. %=(160÷800)×100=20%

Adding decimals: line up the decimal points.
Multiplying decimals: multiply as whole numbers, then count decimal places.
concepts.note: When multiplying decimals, count the total decimal places in BOTH numbers and put that many in the answer.

worked.q: Convert: (a) 7/8 to decimal and %  (b) 0.64 to fraction  (c) 35% to fraction
Find 28% of GH₵650.
A shirt costs GH₵85. It is reduced by 20%. What is the new price?

objectives: Count forwards and backwards to 1,000
Read and write numbers up to 1,000 in words and figures
Identify the value of each digit (hundreds, tens, units)
Compare numbers using >, < and = signs

concepts.heading: Place Value — Hundreds, Tens and Units
concepts.body:
Every number has digits in different POSITIONS. Each position has a value.

Example: The number 347
3 is in the HUNDREDS place → value = 300
4 is in the TENS place → value = 40
7 is in the UNITS place → value = 7

So 347 = 300 + 40 + 7
concepts.note: Remember: Hundreds | Tens | Units — from left to right.

concepts.heading: Reading, Writing & Comparing Numbers to 1,000
concepts.body:
Numbers in figures → write in words:
356 → Three hundred and fifty-six
908 → Nine hundred and eight
1,000 → One thousand

Comparing numbers: always start from the HUNDREDS digit.
456 > 389 because 4 hundreds > 3 hundreds

Use > (greater than), < (less than), = (equal to)
concepts.note: When writing numbers in words, use 'and' after the hundreds: 'three hundred AND fifty-six'.

worked.q: Write the value of each digit in 748.
Write 625 in words.
Which is greater: 483 or 438? Use the correct symbol.
worked.a: Both have 4 hundreds. Then compare tens: 8 tens > 3 tens. So 483 > 438.

objectives: Add 2-digit and 3-digit numbers with and without carrying (regrouping)
Subtract 2-digit and 3-digit numbers with and without borrowing
Solve word problems involving addition and subtraction
Check answers using the inverse operation

concepts.heading: Addition with Carrying (Regrouping)
concepts.body:
When the sum of a column is 10 or more, we CARRY the tens digit to the next column.

Example: 365 + 247
Units: 5 + 7 = 12. Write 2, carry 1.
Tens: 6 + 4 + 1 (carried) = 11. Write 1, carry 1.
Hundreds: 3 + 2 + 1 (carried) = 6.
Answer: 612

Always add from RIGHT to LEFT (units first, then tens, then hundreds).
concepts.note: Check your answer by adding again or by using subtraction.

concepts.heading: Subtraction with Borrowing
concepts.body:
When the top digit is smaller than the bottom digit, we BORROW from the next column.

Example: 423 - 178
Units: 3 < 8. Borrow from tens: 13 - 8 = 5.
Tens: 1 (was 2, gave 1) < 7. Borrow from hundreds: 11 - 7 = 4.
Hundreds: 3 (was 4, gave 1) - 1 = 2.
Answer: 245

Check: 245 + 178 = 423 ✓
concepts.note: KEY WORD CLUES: 'total', 'altogether', 'sum' = addition. 'difference', 'left', 'remaining', 'less' = subtraction.

worked.a: Units: 6+8=14, write 4, carry 1. Tens: 5+7+1=13, write 3, carry 1. Hundreds: 4+3+1=8. Answer: 834.

worked.q: Subtract: 700 - 364
worked.a: Units: 0<4, borrow. 10-4=6. Tens: 9<6... borrow from hundreds: 10-6=4. Hundreds: 6-3=3. Answer: 336. Check: 336+364=700 ✓

worked.q: Ivan collected 247 stamps and his brother collected 185 stamps. How many do they have altogether?

objectives: Recall multiplication facts for the 2× to 10× tables
Use multiplication as repeated addition
Multiply a 2-digit number by a 1-digit number
Solve word problems using multiplication

concepts.heading: Multiplication as Repeated Addition
concepts.body:
Multiplication means adding the same number over and over.
4 × 3 means "4 groups of 3" or 3 + 3 + 3 + 3 = 12
7 × 5 means "7 groups of 5" or 5 + 5 + 5 + 5 + 5 + 5 + 5 = 35

Important rules:
• Any number × 0 = 0. (9 × 0 = 0)
• Any number × 1 = that number. (7 × 1 = 7)
• Order does not matter: 4 × 6 = 6 × 4 = 24
concepts.note: Practise your tables until they are automatic — this will help in ALL areas of maths.

concepts.heading: Multiplying a 2-digit Number by a 1-digit Number
concepts.body:
Method: Split the 2-digit number into tens and units.

Example: 34 × 6
Step 1: Multiply units: 4 × 6 = 24. Write 4, carry 2.
Step 2: Multiply tens: 3 × 6 = 18. Add carried 2: 18 + 2 = 20.
Answer: 204

Another way — expanded method:
34 × 6 = (30 × 6) + (4 × 6) = 180 + 24 = 204

worked.q: Multiply: 47 × 5
worked.a: Units: 7×5=35. Write 5, carry 3. Tens: 4×5=20. Add 3: 23. Answer: 235.

worked.q: A classroom has 8 rows of chairs. Each row has 9 chairs. How many chairs in total?

day7.topic: Division: Sharing Equally & The Relationship with Multiplication
objectives: Understand division as equal sharing and as repeated subtraction
Use the relationship between multiplication and division
Divide a 2-digit number by a 1-digit number
Solve word problems involving division

concepts.heading: Division as Equal Sharing
concepts.body:
Division means sharing equally into groups.
24 ÷ 6 = ? means: share 24 equally into 6 groups. Each group gets 4.

Division and multiplication are INVERSE operations — they undo each other:
If 6 × 4 = 24, then 24 ÷ 6 = 4 AND 24 ÷ 4 = 6

CHECK a division answer using multiplication:
72 ÷ 8 = 9. Check: 9 × 8 = 72 ✓
concepts.note: You cannot divide by ZERO. Division by 0 is undefined.

concepts.heading: Dividing a 2-digit Number by a 1-digit Number
concepts.body:
Example: 84 ÷ 4
Step 1: Divide the tens digit: 8 ÷ 4 = 2. Write 2 above the tens.
Step 2: Divide the units digit: 4 ÷ 4 = 1. Write 1 above the units.
Answer: 21. Check: 21 × 4 = 84 ✓

Example with remainder: 47 ÷ 5
How many times does 5 go into 47? 5 × 9 = 45. Remainder = 47 - 45 = 2.
Answer: 9 remainder 2 (written as 9 r 2)

worked.q: Divide: 76 ÷ 4
48 mangoes are shared equally among 6 children. How many does each child get?

day9.topic: Fractions: Halves, Quarters, Thirds & Simple Fractions
objectives: Understand a fraction as equal parts of a whole
Read and write simple fractions: ½, ¼, ¾, ⅓, ⅔
Compare simple fractions
Find a fraction of a quantity

concepts.heading: What is a Fraction?
concepts.body:
A fraction is a part of a WHOLE that has been divided into EQUAL parts.

A fraction has two parts:
• NUMERATOR (top): how many parts you have
• DENOMINATOR (bottom): how many EQUAL parts the whole is divided into

Example: ¾ means the whole is cut into 4 equal parts and you have 3 of them.

Important fractions to know:
½ = one half (2 equal parts, take 1)
¼ = one quarter (4 equal parts, take 1)
¾ = three quarters (4 equal parts, take 3)
⅓ = one third (3 equal parts, take 1)
concepts.note: The denominator shows how many EQUAL parts. If the parts are not equal, it is NOT a fraction.

concepts.heading: Finding a Fraction of a Quantity
concepts.body:
To find a fraction of a number:
DIVIDE by the denominator, then MULTIPLY by the numerator.

Example: Find ¾ of 24.
Step 1: Divide by denominator: 24 ÷ 4 = 6
Step 2: Multiply by numerator: 6 × 3 = 18
Answer: ¾ of 24 = 18

Example: Find ⅓ of 30.
30 ÷ 3 = 10. So ⅓ of 30 = 10.
concepts.note: 'OF' in maths means MULTIPLY. So ¾ of 24 = ¾ × 24.

worked.q:
What fraction is shaded?
(a) A shape cut into 4 equal parts with 1 shaded.
(b) A shape cut into 6 equal parts with 5 shaded.
Find: (a) ½ of 48  (b) ¼ of 36  (c) ⅓ of 27
In a class of 32 pupils, ¾ are present today. How many pupils are present?
